scope: This course is designed to impart a fundamental knowledge on the preparatory pharmacy with arts and science of preparing the different conventional dosage forms.
objectives: Know the history of profession of pharmacy.
Understand the basics of different dosage forms, pharmaceutical incompatibilities and pharmaceutical calculations.
Understand the professional way of handling the prescription.
Preparation of various conventional dosage forms.
courseOutcomes: CO1: Trace the history of pharmacy and describe various official pharmacopoeias.
CO2: Categorize different dosage forms and understand prescription handling and posology.
CO3: Perform basic pharmaceutical calculations and formulate powders and monophasic liquid dosage forms.
CO4: Design and evaluate biphasic liquid dosage forms (suspensions, emulsions) and suppositories.
CO5: Recognize pharmaceutical incompatibilities and design semisolid dosage forms.

scope: Compounding and dispensing of various conventional dosage forms in accordance with official standards.
objectives: Perform simple compounding and dispensing of conventional dosage forms.
Standardize calculations for syrups, elixirs, linctuses, and emulsions.
Evaluate physical characteristics of suppositories and semisolid preparations.
courseOutcomes: CO1: Formulate and dispense syrups, elixirs, and linctuses following standard pharmacopoeias.
CO2: Prepare and package monophasic solutions and biphasic suspensions/emulsions.
CO3: Compounding of powders, granules, and effervescent formulations.
CO4: Prepare, mold, and evaluate suppositories.
CO5: Formulate semisolid ointments, pastes, gels, and mouthwashes.

scope: This subject deals with the monographs of inorganic drugs and pharmaceuticals.
objectives: Know the sources of impurities and methods to determine the impurities in inorganic drugs and pharmaceuticals.
Understand the medicinal and pharmaceutical importance of inorganic compounds.
courseOutcomes: CO1: Discuss history of pharmacopoeia and perform limit tests for impurities in pharmaceutical compounds.
CO2: Formulate buffers, evaluate isotonic solutions, and understand the role of dental products.
CO3: Classify and explain gastrointestinal agents like acidifiers, antacids, and cathartics.
CO4: Discuss miscellaneous compounds like expectorants, emetics, haematinics, poisons/antidotes, and astringents.
CO5: Understand radioactivity, measurement of radiation, and applications of radiopharmaceuticals.

scope: Experimental methods of performing limit tests, identification tests, purity tests, and inorganic preparation protocols.
objectives: Learn standard limit tests for various ions.
Gain practical skill in identifying inorganic compounds.
Test purity and prepare inorganic pharmaceuticals.
courseOutcomes: CO1: Perform limit tests for chlorides, sulphates, iron, heavy metals, lead, and arsenic.
CO2: Carry out systematic identification tests for inorganic cations and anions.
CO3: Test the purity and swelling/neutralizing capacity of bentonite and aluminum hydroxide gel.
CO4: Compounding and preparation of standard inorganic pharmaceuticals.
